content.thinking:
Let me carefully review all the documents provided before making observations. I'll work through each review perspective systematically.

**Step 1: Project Context**

- Project: Bonfire House, Chatham County, NC
- Code: 2018 North Carolina Residential Code (adopts IRC 2015 with amendments)
- Building type: Single-family residence, R-3 occupancy, Construction Type V-B
- Climate Zone: 4A (stated in code summary)
- Scope: Architectural drawings only (G0.01, G0.11, G1.00, A1.00-A1.01, A2.00-A2.03, A2.01A-B, A3.01-A3.03, A3.10, A3.20-A3.22, A4.00-A4.01, A4.10-A4.13, A5.01, A5.10, A5.20, A5.30, A6.01, A7.01-A7.02, A7.10, A8.01).

The structural, MEP, and civil drawings aren't included, though I do have an 11-page specifications document to reference.

**A. Internal Consistency Across Sheets**

Now I'm checking the stair configurations across the sections. The drawings show two separate stair locations—one on the east side and one on the south side—but I'm noticing discrepancies in how the risers and treads are documented between these sections.

Looking at the riser heights more carefully, I see the east stair section labels a riser height of 6-11/16", which when multiplied by 10 risers gives me roughly 66-11/16"—significantly less than the 10'-0" floor-to-floor height, so there's a calculation or labeling issue I need to resolve. But that math doesn't work either—10 risers at 6-11/16" each only gives me 5'-6-7/8", not the 10'-0" floor-to-floor height I need. Let me look at the section more carefully. The stairs east section appears to span from the crawl space at 502'-0" all the way up to Level 2 at 518'-0", which suggests it might be showing two separate flights rather than one continuous run.

Looking at the section annotations, the east stairs show 10 equal risers with 9 equal treads, while the south stairs show 8 equal risers with 7 equal treads. Now I need to check the floor plans to see which staircase goes where—the north wing appears to have stairs connecting Level 1 to Level 2 near the garage area. So the crawl space is at 502'-0" and Level 1 is at 508'-0", giving a 6'-foot rise. For the south stairs with 8 risers, that works out to 9 inches per riser, which actually exceeds the IRC maximum of 8-3/4 inches. But looking at Section Stairs South on A3.22, it shows 8 equal risers and 7 equal treads between Level 1 at 508'-0" and Level 2 at 518'-0", so I need to recalculate the floor-to-floor height for that section.

Wait, that's a 10-foot rise with 8 risers, which gives 15 inches per riser—that can't be right. I think I'm confusing which stair sections these drawings are showing. Let me look at the plans more carefully. The crawl space has an up stair to Level 1, Level 1 has both up and down stairs, and Level 2 has down stairs. There seem to be two separate stair systems here: the main interior stair between the levels near the entry, and exterior stairs going to the pool deck. The sections labeled on A3.22 are for the east and south stairs, so I need to figure out which flights they're actually depicting. Looking at the section details more carefully, I see the stair from crawl space (502) to level 1 (508) is 6'-0", which would work with the riser dimensions shown, but there's still confusion about how the stairs continue upward and what the floor-to-floor heights actually are. The geometry isn't adding up cleanly based on what I can extract from the drawings. Now I'm checking the window schedule against the floor plans to see if there are any mismatches there. '-6" x 6'-0" FIXED" with count 6. Looking at the floor plans, "9" windows appear in various locations. Let me check if the count seems right.

On A2.01A (South plan), keynotes show "9 9 9" appearing in multiple locations. On A2.01B (North plan), no "9" windows visible. On A2.02 (Level 2), "9" doesn't appear explicitly.

Door 06-2 is listed in the schedule as a 3'-0" × 7'-0" interior door with wood rail and stile plus frosted glass panel, and the kitchen elevation references the same WD-1 frame detail at this opening, which appears to be the transition between the kitchen and master bedroom area. For the Reading Nook (Room 10), the finish schedule shows maple or birch wood with clear stain on three walls, and I'm checking the detail drawing to see how this is specified.

There's a discrepancy with the Reading Nook walls—the detail drawing shows T&G boards (WD-4) but the finish schedule lists WD-1, which is interior trim rather than the full wall treatment. For the garage door 15-1, the schedule calls for an 18'-0" × 8'-0" metal door, and I need to verify this dimension against the elevation drawings to ensure it fits the opening.

I'm also noticing incomplete references in the room finish schedule—the Master Bathroom and another room have notes directing to shower finishes on another sheet, but the sheet numbers aren't filled in.

The Attic (Room 20) is listed as unfinished plywood sheathing on all surfaces, which seems minimal if it's meant to be a usable space rather than just storage. And I'm checking Door 10-1 in the Reading Nook—the schedule lists it, but I'm not seeing clear evidence on the plans that this built-in bunk area actually has a separate door opening.

Looking at the door schedule, 10-1 is listed as an interior solid-core wood door at 3'-0" × 7'-0", which seems reasonable if there is indeed a door there. The Powder Room (Room 14) is flagged as a potential issue—the finish schedule shows carpet (F-4) for the floor, which doesn't make sense in a moisture-prone space and is likely a mistake for tile or similar. The Entry Hall confirms F-2 tile thinset with honed limestone, which aligns with the specifications.

Now I'm noticing a significant conflict with the windows: the schedule references Marvin product codes like ESDG2020 and ESCA2020, but the specs call for Andersen 400 Series windows. These are different manufacturers entirely, so this needs to be resolved. I'm also cross-referencing the wall assembly tags from the floor plans against the wall assembly details—the exterior walls are tagged E-1 through E-4 on the plans, and I'm checking what each of those corresponds to in the assembly details.

Looking at how E-1 (CMU exterior wall) appears in both the crawl space plan and Level 1, I'm verifying this makes sense by checking the elevation and wall sections. The south elevation and entry hall details do show CMU at the base, so the tagging appears consistent, though I want to make sure the CMU placement is correct throughout.

Now checking the floor assemblies—F-1 is the typical wood floor system with joists and gypsum board, while F-5 is the garage-specific concrete slab assembly. The room finish schedule confirms Room 15 uses F-5, and the enlarged plan shows the concrete control joints tagged correctly. However, I'm noticing the F-5 detail only lists the 4" concrete slab and vapor barrier without mentioning a subbase, which seems incomplete compared to what the specifications might require.

Looking at the roof assemblies, there's a callout for R-2 on the west elevation, but the horizontal assemblies sheet only defines R-1. That R-2 designation appears to be missing from the details entirely. For the exterior stone assembly F-6, it's properly layered with the stone veneer, thinset, concrete slab, and subbase components, and the spec cross-references specific room locations for where this assembly applies. ), Powder Room (14). But the room finish schedule for Laundry Room (12) shows F-2 (Tile Thinset) not F-6 (Exterior Stone). F-6 shows a concrete slab + subbase assembly which seems exterior-oriented. This could be a conflict - Laundry Room may have interior tile (F-2) not exterior stone (F-6).

Actually wait - looking again: spec says SS-2 is at "Entry Hall (1), Laundry Room (12), Powder Room (14)." The room finish schedule shows:
- Room 01 (Entry Hall): Floor F-2 ← consistent with SS-2 on F-2 (tile thinset interior application)
- Room 12 (Laundry Room): Floor F-2 ← same
- Room 14 (Powder Room): Floor F-4 (CARPET) ← conflict! Spec says SS-2 at Powder Room 14, but schedule shows carpet instead. That's a clear mismatch.

There's also a clarity issue with the "SIM" callouts scattered across the plans - they reference details like A4.01 but don't specify which assembly within that detail they're actually similar to. And then there's the Reading Nook situation where Door 10-1 appears in the schedule, but the plan shows it as more of an alcove or built-in rather than a separate room that would need a door.

Looking at the countertop and backsplash specs, there's a minor discrepancy: the spec lists "Silestone Ethereal Glow Polished" for both but doesn't specify thickness (says "TBD"), yet the kitchen elevation drawing shows the backsplash as 6mm. And in the Master Bath elevation, the vanity references WD-1 bathroom cabinets, which the spec does confirm, but I need to verify the full details match up.

Now checking the closet callouts and light fixture schedule—there's a keynote "A" for shelf and pole dimensions that appears consistently across closet locations, which looks correct. But I'm noticing an inconsistency with the L7 strip lighting: the fixture schedule lists it as a single item with count "2," yet on the detailed plans it's called out in multiple different locations (under counter, at base, under top bunk, under railing) with the spec mentioning 75 linear feet total for kitchen and stair handrails. The schedule seems to be undercounting the actual applications.

Similarly, L8 step lights are scheduled as 12 units specifically for pool steps, but the drawings show step lights at deck stairs and potentially other locations too, so that count might be incomplete. The section references I'm checking appear to align properly between the plan callouts and the actual sections on A3.10. Now I need to look at wall assembly W-3 to see if there are any other discrepancies.

W-3 is supposed to be the insulated interior wall with 2x6 studs and gypsum board totaling 6 inches nominal, but I'm not seeing it consistently tagged throughout the plans. The general note says W1 is typical unless otherwise noted, so W-3 should be clearly marked where it's used. I found one W-3 notation near the laundry and garage area on A2.01B, but I need to verify if that's the only location or if there are others I'm missing.

Now looking at the pool deck gates—22-1 and 22-2 are 3'-0" by 4'-0" exterior wood gates with cable and stained finish. They're shown on the deck plan and elevations, and the 4'-0" height aligns with the 48-inch cable railing system. The spec also mentions a central vacuum system with 4 wall fixtures, though I need to track down where those are located in the plans.

Shifting to dimensional checks now. The east stair shows 10 equal risers over 9 equal treads spanning a 10-foot floor-to-floor height, which would put each riser at 12 inches—well above the IRC maximum of 7¾ inches, so that's a problem I need to flag.

But wait, I'm seeing "6 11/16"" marked as the actual riser height on the section, which changes things. If each riser is really 6-11/16 inches, then 10 risers only gets me to about 5 feet 7 inches, not the full 10-foot span. So either the riser count is off, there's a landing I'm missing, or this section is showing a partial stair run rather than the full floor-to-floor height. I need to look more carefully at what A3.22 is actually depicting. Looking at the south stair now, it shows 8 equal risers and 7 equal treads between Level 1 and Level 2, which is a 10-foot rise. That would mean each riser is 15 inches—way too tall and non-compliant with code. There's definitely something off with how these stairs are dimensioned or what levels they're actually connecting. the east stair section from Level 1 to Level 2 spans 120 inches, which with 10 risers gives 12 inches per riser—clearly non-compliant. Looking back at the section drawing, I can see the level datums and what appear to be dimension callouts, though I need to parse exactly what those measurements represent.

If the labeled riser height is actually 6-11/16", that's technically within code but would only total about 5'7" for 10 risers, nowhere near the 10-foot floor-to-floor height. This suggests the stair section might be showing something else entirely—possibly an exterior stair or a descent to the crawl space rather than the main interior stair between floors.

Looking at the south stair section with 8 risers between Level 1 and Level 2, if those levels are truly 10 feet apart, that would require 15-inch risers, which violates code. The discrepancy might resolve if Level 2 isn't actually 10 feet above Level 1 at this particular stair location, perhaps due to a sloped floor or other condition I'm not seeing clearly. The stair geometry is definitely problematic with those 15-inch risers. Now I'm checking the overall building dimensions across the plans—Level 1 shows roughly 72'-1-1/2" while the crawl space plan indicates 70'-3", and these discrepancies need to be documented even though the plans cover different areas.

The building height checks out consistently at 27'-11" between the code summary and the east elevation, but I should verify the crawl space level designation and how it relates to the overall datum.

Looking at the crawl space elevations across the sheets, there's a discrepancy—the east and north elevations show 501'-0" while the west elevation shows 502'-0", which is a 1-foot variance that needs clarification.

The roof has multiple pitches documented: 3"/12", 2"/12", and 1/4"/12" on different areas per the plan, and the elevations confirm the sloped geometry, though I should verify these match the structural coordination.

The entry hall section on A3.20 has dimension callouts that appear to be cut off—I need to see the complete measurements to assess them properly.

Looking at the window specifications, there's a direct conflict: the spec calls for Andersen 400 Series Casement & Fixed windows, but the window schedule on A8.01 lists Marvin Ultrex products with specific series codes instead.

For the exterior doors, the spec references Jeld Wen IWP Wood doors with glass panels, yet the door schedule doesn't specify any manufacturer—just the material types. The schedule is missing that manufacturer detail.

On insulation, the spec details a wall assembly of R21 fiberglass batts plus R6 insulated ZIP board totaling R27, which aligns with what the code summary shows, though I need to verify the complete wall assembly matches across both documents. I'm noticing the dimensions don't quite add up—the drawing shows a 9" total wall thickness, but when I calculate the layers (1/2" gypsum + 5-1/2" studs + 1" rigid + 1/2" plywood + 3/4" exterior), I get 8-1/4". More importantly, there's a mismatch between what the spec calls for—"1" Insulated ZIP Board" for R6—and what the drawing actually shows, which is 1-1/2" rigid insulation, two different products entirely.

Looking at the crawl space vapor barrier requirement, the spec is clear about needing 20mil with string reinforcement, but the wall assembly drawing for the CMU/crawl space just shows "VAPOR BARRIER" without those specifications—that's a gap in the drawing details.

The hydronic radiant floor system appears consistent between the spec and plan—both reference it for the Master Bathroom only, with the plan showing a note about the bidder designing the layout.

For the countertop and backsplash, there's a discrepancy: the spec lists the thickness as TBD for both the Silestone Ethereal Glow countertop and backsplash, but the elevation drawing calls out 6mm specifically for the backsplash. That's a minor conflict that needs clarification.

Now I'm checking the exterior wood cladding—the spec specifies WD-3 Thermory Ash for the South and West facades, but I need to verify if the wall assembly drawings actually show this cladding in those locations.

Looking at the wall assemblies, E-3 is a fiberboard option while E-4 is the wood cladding assembly. The elevations show vertical siding on the south facade (which would be wood) and horizontal lap siding on the north facade (which would be fiberboard). But here's the conflict: the spec says Hardieplank lap siding goes on the North and West facades, while also specifying WD-3 wood cladding for the West facade. The spec is contradicting itself on what material should be on the west facade.

Now looking at the soffit details, the spec calls for Hardiesoffit panels but the drawings show fiberboard soffit instead, which is another material mismatch between the documents. Hardiesoffit is a fiber cement product, while "fiberboard" could be confused with wood-based fiberboard. The drawings say "fiberboard" while the spec says "Hardiesoffit." These may refer to the same product (Hardiesoffit is a fiber cement board) but the terminology is inconsistent and potentially confusing.

The foundation drain spec calls for a 4" perforated pipe with filter fabric on 4" of #67 aggregate, but this detail isn't shown in the architectural drawings—it's likely in the structural/civil drawings that weren't provided, though the architectural set should still coordinate this information.

For the interior wood T&G, I'm clarifying the labeling: W-3 and W-4 are wall assembly types, not wood types. WD-4 refers to the interior T&G boards specified as 1x6 maple, and it appears on both the W-3 (Interior Wall, Insulated) and W-4 (Interior Wall, 2X4 Wood Veneer) assemblies as wood blocking components, though the exact application and coordination between these walls needs verification.

Now I'm looking at the garage door specification—it describes a Clopay Modern Steel door with long panel windows and glazing, but the door schedule just lists it as "METAL / WOOD / PAINTED" without noting the glazing or the 2 remote openers and keypad mentioned in the spec. There's also a carpet location conflict where the spec restricts carpet to the master closet only under floor assembly F-4, but the room finish schedule shows F-4 carpet in multiple locations.

Moving into the building envelope details, the crawl space moisture protection setup shows a vapor barrier on the interior CMU face and parge coat below grade, but there's no exterior waterproofing layer specified—just the parge coat, which may be insufficient for a climate zone 4A area with heavy rainfall like North Carolina.

The exterior wall assemblies in E-4 and E-3 both incorporate a rainscreen design with pressure-treated furring creating an air gap behind the cladding, which is well-suited for this climate. The roof assembly follows a logical sequence from outside in: standing seam metal, air gap, rigid insulation, plywood, joists with insulation, and interior gypsum—all properly ordered for moisture management and thermal performance. Wait - the rigid insulation is between the air gap and the plywood, which means the air gap sits above it against the metal roof. So the rigid insulation is correctly positioned on the exterior side of the plywood sheathing, and the air gap provides ventilation above it. But I'm noticing the assembly doesn't specify a weather barrier or underlayment between the metal roof and the rigid insulation, which is typically required.

Now looking at the E-4 wall assembly, I'm seeing the rigid insulation positioned between the plywood sheathing and the studs - that's on the interior side of the sheathing, which is problematic for moisture management since you want the rigid insulation on the exterior to create a proper thermal and moisture barrier. The rigid insulation placement is the real issue here—it's on the interior side of the plywood rather than the exterior, which leaves the sheathing vulnerable to condensation in cold climates like CZ 4A. Let me look more carefully at the actual drawing dimensions to see what's really specified, since the layer breakdown needs to account for the full 9-inch assembly. outside to inside: WD-3 / furring(air gap) / weather barrier / rigid / plywood / studs+insulation / gypsum. 

Wait, this puts the rigid between the weather barrier and the plywood. So the layer sequence exterior-to-interior is: Cladding → Air gap → Weather barrier → 1.5" Rigid → Plywood sheathing → Stud cavity with insulation → Gypsum.

This puts the weather barrier on the OUTSIDE of the rigid insulation, between the air gap and the rigid. That's unusual - typically weather barrier goes between the rigid and the cladding/furring, not between the rigid and the plywood. Having the weather barrier on the exterior face of the rigid (between rigid and air gap) is one approach, but more commonly the weather barrier is on the exterior face of the sheathing.

Now I'm reconsidering the layer order from the drawing dimensions. If I'm reading the measurements correctly—9" total with 5½" studs, 1½" rigid, ½" plywood, ¾" furring, and ¾" WD-3—then working from inside out, the sequence would be gypsum, studs, rigid insulation, plywood, furring, and WD-3 cladding. With the rigid on the exterior side of the plywood, the sheathing stays warmer in cold weather, which helps with moisture management. The outboard rigid approach keeps the sheathing warm, which is the right way to handle this from a building science perspective. Without a clearer image, I can't definitively pin down the layer sequence, so I'll flag that as something to verify. Looking at the crawl space insulation now—the drawing shows 1/2" rigid at the interior face, but the code summary and spec both call for R10, which requires 2" of XPS. That's a meaningful gap between what's drawn and what's specified. The roof underlayment details are cut off, but I need to check those too. The stair riser height is problematic—with only 8 risers across a 10-foot rise, each would be 15 inches, which violates the IRC maximum of 7-3/4 inches. For the garage separation, the floor assembly has gypsum board on the garage side which addresses the fire rating requirement, but I need to verify that the walls between the garage and adjacent spaces (office, hallway, laundry) are properly detailed on the floor plan. , the garage adjoins the office and laundry. Are these walls separating garage from conditioned space shown with appropriate gypsum on the garage side?

Actually the code summary says 0 HR for all elements. For an IRC dwelling, the garage separation requirement is per IRC R302.6: "The garage shall be separated from the residence and its attic area by not less than ½-inch gypsum board..." This isn't a fire "rating" per se but a material requirement. The code summary listing 0 HR may be misleading, as the separation requirement still exists per IRC.

3. **Handrail/guardrail**: The enlarged plan shows "36" CABLE RAILING" at certain locations and "48" CABLE RAILING" at the pool deck. For interior guardrails (e.g., at the Level 2 landing), the IRC requires minimum 36" guardrail height for areas open more than 30" above the floor. The pool deck's 48" railing is appropriate for the drop-off, but I need to verify the 36" cable railing is actually at interior locations where that height is sufficient.

4. **Egress from sleeping rooms**: Sleeping rooms need emergency escape openings, and I'm checking whether the bedrooms on both levels have adequate egress. The Master Bedroom has an exterior door to the deck, which works, but I need to trace which windows on the Second Bedroom and Guest Bedroom face outside and whether they're operable casement windows rather than fixed units.

5. **Stair handrail height**: The section drawing shows the handrail at 3'-0", but IRC requires interior stair handrails to be 34"-38" above the stair nosing, so I need to confirm this measurement is correct.

6. **Crawl space access**: The 5'-0" × 5'-0" exterior metal door for the crawl space far exceeds the IRC minimum of 16"×24", so that's compliant.

7. **Stair width**: The plans don't explicitly dimension the stair width in the section drawings, but IRC requires a minimum 36" clear width that should be verified.

8. **Porch overhang structure**: The specification mentions structural steel framing with tube steel columns hidden inside the wall framing for the south porch overhang, which I'm reviewing against the details shown on the sections.

9. **R-2 roof assembly**: The west elevation references an "R-2" roof assembly, but the horizontal assemblies sheet only defines R-1. This undefined assembly needs to be clarified in the drawing set.

10. **Pool deck structure**: The deck sections repeatedly defer to the structural drawings for the elevated deck above the above-ground pool, so I need to see those structural details to fully evaluate the design.

11. **In-floor hydronic heating system**: The notes reference an in-floor hydronic heating system that I'm checking for coordination with the structural and mechanical systems.

12. **Corner window framing**: The reading nook and kitchen have corner windows meeting at the building corner, and while the jamb details show how the windows connect, the structural post or framing required at that corner intersection needs to be detailed and coordinated.

13. **Solar array installation**: The roof plan shows a solar array, and I'm looking for the installation details and specifications.

14. **Standing seam roof walkability**: The spec requires the roof to be walkable with proper underlayment or gage thickness, but the roof assembly doesn't address this requirement.

15. **Aquor House Hydrant rough-ins**: The elevations call out multiple exterior hose bibs as "AQUOR HOUSE HYDRANT V2+" but the wall penetration and rough-in details for these fixtures are missing from the drawings.

16. **Garage slab at door threshold**: The garage door base condition shows the concrete slab, and I need to check how the threshold is detailed.

17. **Wood deck to CMU wall connection**: The entry hall shows a deck connecting to a CMU extension wall with a note about the foundation, but the actual ledger fastening detail to the CMU isn't fully shown.

18. **R-2 roof assembly**: It's referenced on one sheet but not actually defined on the roof details sheet.

19. **Room finish schedule references**: Several rooms have incomplete notes that say "FOR SHOWER FINISHES SEE" without completing the reference.

20. **Multiple TBD items throughout the spec**: Interior paint colors, carpet material, hardwood flooring, kitchen sink, countertop thickness, and hardware are all marked TBD as owner decisions. While acceptable for a bid set, it creates uncertainty. Similarly, all lighting fixtures show TBD for manufacturer and model with no allowance amounts specified.

21. **Hydronic system design approach**: The spec indicates a "bidder design" approach for the hydronic system, which needs clarification on scope and performance requirements.

22. **Sheet index discrepancy**: I'm checking the sheet list against what's actually provided—there's some confusion around the deck-related sheets (A5.10, A5.11, A5.20, A5.30) and whether all referenced sheets are actually included. The cross-references within the drawings themselves point to A5.20, not A5.11, which confirms the sheet numbering is inconsistent with the cover list. Additionally, the spec references a sheet A8.10 for the custom bunk bed nook details, but that sheet doesn't exist in the drawing set index—only A8.01 is listed. So I'm seeing multiple missing or mismatched sheet numbers that need to be reconciled.

Looking at the energy code compliance, the window specs show U=.32 and SHGC=.20, which do meet the code thresholds of U≤.35 and SHGC≤.3, but there's a discrepancy: the code summary cites Andersen 400 Series windows while the actual window schedule specifies Marvin windows. The pool mechanical layout is also problematic because the spec defers the technical specifications to be determined later with the subcontractor and owner, leaving significant design work unresolved at the bid phase. And on the reflected ceiling plan, I'm noticing clarity issues that need attention.

The reflected ceiling plan on A6.01 shows the entry hall with vented fiberboard soffit and notes a "WD-4" ceiling designation in the spec, but the plan itself doesn't clearly differentiate between wood and gypsum ceiling areas. The return grille notations marked "(IN WALL)" on A2.01A lack corresponding HVAC duct routing in the architectural drawings, which creates coordination concerns for wall penetrations even though MEP drawings aren't provided. The pool deck shows 48" cable railing on three sides, and the spec references "48" Wood & Frame Cable" but the details need verification.

Now I'm noticing some inconsistencies in the door schedules and details—the garage door 15-1 is listed as hollow metal in the schedule but the garage details show an Accoya frame, and the spec calls for WD-2 (Accoya) material while the schedule lists it as painted wood, which creates some ambiguity about what's actually specified.

For window type 10, there's a mismatch: it's a fixed 7'-6" × 1'-6" window that appears to be the south-facing clerestory mentioned in the specs, but the spec calls for motorized blinds on these windows—which doesn't make sense for a fixed window unless the blinds are meant to be installed separately from the window itself.

The spec references six south-facing clerestory windows needing motorized blind prewiring, yet the schedule only shows one type 10 window, suggesting either multiple instances aren't labeled consistently or there's a discrepancy in the count.

I'm also noticing the enlarged plans show 84" dimensions at various openings (consistent with the 7'-0" door heights in the schedule), and the master bathroom calls for a Toto Guinevere Acrylic Air Bath that's 66" wide, which I need to verify fits the layout shown on the north elevation.

Looking at the dimensional details, there's a 45-degree angle condition appearing in the reading nook area between levels and again in the office corner, which I should track as I compile my findings.

Now I'm organizing the internal consistency issues I've identified: the powder room finish schedule conflicts with the specifications, the window schedule specifies Marvin products when Andersen 400 Series is required, the reading nook wall finishes don't align between the schedule and the sections, and there's an incomplete callout for the R-2 roof assembly on the west elevation.

I'm also noting that the L7 strip lighting count appears understated in the fixture schedule despite multiple installation locations throughout the project, the shower finish references in several room schedules are missing their detail numbers, and there's a discrepancy between the sheet list showing A5.11 and the actual drawing set which contains A5.20 instead.

Additionally, the specifications reference a non-existent sheet A8.10 for the custom bunk bed details, the garage door dimensions listed in the schedule don't align with what's shown on the plans and elevations and need field verification, and the window schedule only documents a single window type despite what appears to be multiple window configurations throughout the project.

There's also confusion around wall assembly tags on the crawl space and Level 1 plans — E-1 tags appear in locations where it's unclear if they should reference the crawl space CMU assembly or a different assembly for above-grade conditions. Finally, the Master Bathroom finish schedule lists a tile floor specification that conflicts with what's stated in the project specifications.

The specifications call out a hydronic radiant heating system specifically for the Master Bathroom, and the plan shows this system installed, but the floor assembly detail doesn't include the heating mat or tubing layer, creating a disconnect between the schedule, spec, and actual assembly. Moving into dimensional issues, the stair section shows an 8-riser configuration with a 10-foot floor-to-floor height, which would require 15-inch risers — an impossible and code-violating dimension. There appear to be similar dimensional conflicts in other stair sections as well.

The crawl space elevation is marked differently across the exterior elevation sheets, ranging from 501 to 502 feet, so I need to verify which is correct. The roof assembly detail also seems incomplete or unclear regarding how the insulation layers coordinate with the structural system.

Moving to spec-to-drawing conflicts now: the window schedule calls for Marvin Ultrex models, but the specifications require Andersen 400 Series instead — this is a direct conflict between what's scheduled and what's specified that needs resolution.

There's also a cladding assignment problem where the specifications list both wood cladding for the south and west facades and Hardieplank lap siding for the north and west facades, leaving the west facade ambiguous about which material applies where.

Additionally, the specifications call for Hardiesoffit panels for soffits and porch ceilings, but the exterior details consistently show vented fiberboard soffit instead — these are different material types that don't align.

The crawl space insulation thickness is another issue: the wall assembly shows only 1/2" rigid insulation on the interior CMU face, but the code requirements and specifications both call for 2" XPS rigid insulation to achieve R-10 — what's shown provides only about R-2.5.

The carpet finish designation also conflicts between documents: specifications limit F-4 carpet to the master closet only, yet the room finish schedule assigns F-4 to both the master closet and the powder room.

Finally, there's a mismatch on the laundry room finish material where the specifications specify SS-2 honed limestone, but the room finish schedule appears to show something different.

Looking at the roof assembly, I notice the R-1 detail on A4.01 is missing the typical underlayment layers — there's no membrane shown between the plywood and rigid insulation or between the insulation and the standing seam metal roof, which is standard practice for metal roofing installations.

The backsplash dimension also creates a conflict: the kitchen elevation calls out "SS-1 6MM" specifically, but the project specifications list the backsplash thickness as "TBD," so there's a direct contradiction between the drawing callout and the unresolved specification.

Now I'm moving into the building envelope section.

The E-1 wall assembly for the CMU foundation shows only a parge coat extending 6 inches below grade, but there's no waterproofing membrane or drainage board for the below-grade CMU walls — this is insufficient for moisture control in Climate Zone 4A.

The crawl space insulation is also undersized: the E-1 assembly shows only 1/2 inch of rigid insulation on the interior face (roughly R2.5), but the code requires R10 at crawl space walls and the specifications call for 2 inches of XPS (R10), creating a significant gap between what's drawn and what's required.

The roof assembly R-1 is missing an underlayment layer under the standing seam metal roof, which leaves a moisture protection gap. Additionally, the wall assembly E-4 shows 1-1/2 inches of rigid insulation, but the specifications require R21 cavity insulation plus R6 continuous using Insulated ZIP Board — ZIP Board is a sheathing product that replaces plywood, so the assembly as drawn doesn't match the specified product. Finally, the stair section shows 8 risers over a 10-foot floor-to-floor height, which would require 15-inch risers that far exceed the IRC maximum of 7-3/4 inches per riser.

The Second Bedroom on Level 1 has only fixed windows, but the IRC requires an operable emergency escape and rescue opening for sleeping rooms, and no alternative operable window or exterior door is provided. The Guest Bedroom on Level 2 also lacks clearly identified operable windows that would meet the IRC emergency escape opening requirements. The code summary lists all construction as "0 HR" including floor construction, which may be misleading since the IRC requires minimum physical separation for certain assemblies.

The guardrail height at the main interior stair landing isn't dimensioned anywhere in the set, so I can't verify it meets the 42" minimum required by the IRC for openings over 30" above the floor. The reading nook bunk structure spans from Level 1 to Level 2 with a window in the exterior wall, but the plans don't show how it bears at the floor, connects to the exterior wall, or ties into the Level 2 floor framing above.

The entry porch overhang shows steel tube framing for the canopy but lacks details on how the tubes connect to the wall framing or how the column base is anchored. The solar array on the roof has cables routed to a wall penetration, but there are no mounting, attachment, flashing, or waterproofing details provided. The corner window conditions at the Reading Nook and Kitchen show windows meeting at a corner with no structural post or king stud between the frames, which needs structural support that isn't detailed architecturally or coordinated with the structural drawings.

The hydronic in-floor heating system in the Master Bathroom is marked as "BIDDER DESIGN LAYOUT" with no performance criteria, tubing specifications, or design parameters provided to guide the bidder. There's also a missing sheet reference—A8.10 is cited in the specifications for the reading nook bunk bed detail but doesn't exist in the drawing set. The cover sheet index lists "A5.11 SECTIONS, DECK" but the actual deck sections sheet is numbered A5.20, suggesting either a missing sheet or an untracked renumbering. Additionally, room finish schedule comments for several rooms reference shower finishes without completing the reference.

The R-2 roof assembly shown on elevation A3.01 isn't defined anywhere in the horizontal assemblies or other sheets. The acoustic insulation treatment specified for walls between the Master Bedroom and Second Bedroom, and between the second bathroom and hallway/kitchen, has no corresponding wall section or detail, and none of the wall types W-1 through W-4 are designated as acoustically rated. The lighting fixture schedule lists all manufacturer and model entries as "TBD" with no allowance amount specified.

The specifications mention a geothermal ground source heat pump system under consideration, but the drawings show a conventional split heat pump with no accommodation for geothermal wells — if the owner chooses geothermal, the mechanical room layout and crawl space penetrations would need significant changes. The entry door handleset is specified as "Level Lock" with a URL, but the door schedule lacks a populated hardware column for most doors. The window schedule has an empty comments column for all window types with no notation for screens, egress hardware, or motors.

Now I'm noticing the window schedule doesn't specify which units need motorized blind rough-ins either — that's information that affects how the rough-in and trim coordinate. Looking at the floor assemblies, F-3 at the shower shows a 2×8 joist while most others use 2×10, which means the shower floor steps down intentionally per spec, but the plan should clarify where the framing transitions. I'm also seeing that window types "2" and "2C" are both listed as 2'-0" × 2'-0" with matching counts of 2 each — I should verify if those counts are accurate. On the roof plan, there are multiple roof pits that need closer examination.

The roof assembly shows different pitches — 3"/1'-0", 2"/1'-0", and 1/4"/1'-0" — and where these different pitched roofs meet, the ridge and eave heights need to be coordinated, which could create some geometric complexity. For the mechanical systems, the spec calls for two Rinnai tankless water heaters, and while the crawl space plan notes the hot water location relative to the kitchen and bathrooms, the actual positioning and spacing of both units isn't clearly dimensioned. Looking back at the roof assembly R-1 layer sequence, I'm seeing the order from bottom to top, but I need to double-check what the A4.01 text specifies. Looking back at the R-1 assembly on A4.01, I can see the weather barrier is actually positioned between the plywood and the TJI joists—on the interior side of the plywood rather than the exterior, which is atypical since weather barriers are usually placed on the outside to protect against moisture infiltration. I should flag one more issue about the weather barrier placement for the pool area. The deck has cable railings, but the south edge is marked as open in the specs, which might violate local pool barrier codes depending on jurisdiction. Now I'm organizing all my findings into the required format, making sure each one is distinct and not duplicated—I'll consolidate the window specification conflict under section A since it appears in both the drawing schedule and spec, then reference it in section C to avoid repetition.

content.text:
## Step 1: Project Context

- **Project:** Bonfire House, Chatham County, NC; 2018 NC Residential Code (IRC 2015 with amendments); Chatham County Building Inspections
- **Building type:** Single-family residence; Occupancy R-3; Construction Type V-B
- **Climate zone:** 4A (stated in G0.11)
- **Drawing set scope:** Architectural sheets only — G, A1, A2, A3, A4, A5, A6, A7, A8 series. Structural, MEP, civil, and landscape drawings not included.
- **Specifications:** 11-page project specifications document provided covering all divisions relevant to this project.

---

## Step 2 & 3: Findings

### A — Internal Consistency Across Sheets

A-1 | A8.01 Room Finish Schedule, Room 14 | The Powder Room (Room 14) is assigned floor assembly F-4 (Carpet) in the room finish schedule, which is inappropriate for a wet room and conflicts with the specification statement that carpet is located in the master closet only. | Certain

A-2 | A8.01 Window Schedule | Window schedule assigns Marvin Ultrex product codes (ESDG2020, ESCA2020, ESDG3030, etc.) and lists "Ultrex-Marvin-Stonewhite" as both interior and cladding material for all window types, directly conflicting with the project specifications on page 3 which require Andersen 400 Series windows. | Certain

A-3 | A8.01 Room Finish Schedule, Room 10; A7.10 Sheet 1 | The room finish schedule lists the Reading Nook (Room 10) wall finishes as WD-1 on three sides, but Section A7.10/1 and the project specifications describe WD-4 (1×6 T&G maple boards) as the wall and ceiling material in the reading nook bunk alcove — WD-1 and WD-4 are different products. | Certain

A-4 | A3.01 West Elevation; A4.01 | Roof assembly tag "R-2" is called out on the West Elevation (A3.01) but is not defined anywhere in the horizontal assemblies sheet A4.01, which defines only R-1. | Certain

A-5 | A6.01 Lighting Fixture Schedule | The lighting fixture schedule lists L7 (Strip Lighting) with a count of 2, but L7 is called out in multiple distinct locations throughout the enlarged plans (under-counter kitchen, bunk nook ceiling, stair handrail underside) totaling 75 LF per the project specifications — a count of 2 fixtures is irreconcilable with 75 LF of strip lighting. | Certain

A-6 | A8.01 Room Finish Schedule, Rooms 07, 11, 18 | The Comments column for Master Bathroom (07), Bathroom (11), and Guest Bathroom (18) reads "FOR SHOWER FINISHES SEE" with no sheet or detail reference completing the callout, leaving shower wall and floor finishes unresolvable from the schedule alone. | Certain

A-7 | Cover Sheet (G0.00) Sheet List; Drawing Set | The cover sheet index lists "A5.11 SECTIONS, DECK" but the drawing set contains a sheet numbered A5.20 titled "SECTIONS, DECK"; sheet A5.11 does not exist in the set and A5.20 is not listed in the index, creating an unresolvable cross-reference discrepancy. | Certain

A-8 | Project Specifications, page 5; Drawing Set Index | The project specifications reference "page A8.10" for the custom bunk bed nook detail, but no sheet A8.10 exists in the drawing set index or the provided drawings. | Certain

A-9 | A6.01 Lighting Fixture Schedule; A2.03 Roof Plan | The window schedule (A8.01) shows only one instance of window type 10 (7'-6" × 1'-6" fixed), but the project specifications require prewiring for motorized blinds at six south-facing clerestory windows; neither the window schedule nor the plans account for six clerestory windows of any type. | High

A-10 | A8.01 Room Finish Schedule, Room 12; Specifications page 3 | The project specifications list SS-2 (honed limestone) as a finish for Laundry Room (12), but the room finish schedule assigns floor assembly F-2 (Tile Thinset — an interior ceramic tile assembly on a wood subfloor) to the Laundry Room, not F-6 (Exterior Stone on concrete slab), which is the assembly depicted for SS-2 installation. | High

A-11 | A2.01A Enlarged South Plan; A4.00 | Wall assembly E-1 (Exterior Wall, CMU) is tagged on exterior walls of the south wing at Level 1 on A2.01A, but E-1 is the crawl space CMU assembly; it is not clear from the plans whether CMU construction continues above grade at Level 1 or whether the wall type tag should transition to E-4 at the Level 1 floor line. | High

A-12 | A8.01 Room Finish Schedule, Room 14; A2.01A; Specifications page 3 | The room finish schedule assigns F-4 (Carpet) to the Powder Room (Room 14), but the project specifications state that SS-2 (honed limestone) is the finish for the Powder Room — two incompatible floor materials are assigned to the same room in different project documents. | Certain

A-13 | A2.01A Enlarged South Plan; A4.01 F-2 Assembly | The Master Bathroom (Room 07) floor is shown as F-2 (plywood subfloor + thinset + tile) in the room finish schedule and the enlarged plan notes an in-floor hydronic heating system with "BIDDER DESIGN LAYOUT," but the F-2 floor assembly on A4.01 does not include a heating mat or tubing layer, leaving the hydronic system thermally and structurally uncoordinated with the floor assembly. | High

---

### B — Dimensional and Geometric Integrity

B-1 | A3.22 Sheet 2, Section Stairs South | The south stair section shows Level 1 datum at 508'-0" and Level 2 datum at 518'-0" (10'-0" floor-to-floor) with "8 EQ RISERS," which would require a riser height of 15" — geometrically impossible and more than double the maximum IRC riser height of 7-3/4". | Certain

B-2 | A3.22 Sheet 1, Section Stairs East | The east stair section shows "10 EQ RISERS / 9 EQ TREADS" between the Level 1 and Level 2 datums (10'-0" floor-to-floor); with a labeled riser height of 6-11/16", 10 risers yield a total rise of approximately 5'-7", which does not match the 10'-0" floor-to-floor distance — the section datum labels and riser count are irreconcilable as drawn. | Certain

B-3 | A3.01, A3.02, A3.03, A3.10 | The crawl space floor datum elevation is inconsistently labeled across exterior elevation and section sheets: A3.10 Section North shows "502'-0"," A3.02 North Elevation shows "501'-0"," A3.03 West Elevation Partial shows "502'-0"" — the crawl space datum varies by 1'-0" between sheets. | Certain

B-4 | A2.03 Roof Plan | The roof plan shows at least three distinct roof pitches (3"/1'-0", 2"/1'-0", and 1/4"/1'-0") meeting at ridge and valley intersections, but no ridge height elevations or valley elevations are dimensioned on the roof plan, leaving the geometric coordination of intersecting roof planes unresolvable from the architectural drawings. | High

B-5 | A3.20 Sheet 4, Section Typical | The typical wall section (A3.20/4) labels the ceiling height dimension as "VARIES" without providing a minimum or maximum value or referencing where the ceiling height is established, making it impossible to confirm finish ceiling heights in the main living wing from the documents. | High

---

### C — Specification-to-Drawing Alignment

C-1 | A8.01 Window Schedule; Specifications page 3 | The window schedule specifies Marvin Ultrex product codes and Ultrex-Marvin-Stonewhite framing for all window types, while the project specifications explicitly require Andersen 400 Series Casement and Fixed windows — a direct and irreconcilable product conflict between the two primary contract documents. | Certain

C-2 | Specifications pages 2–3 | The project specifications assign Exterior Wood Cladding WD-3 (Thermory Ash) to "South and West Facades" and simultaneously assign Hardieplank Lap Siding to "North and West Facades," with both products assigned to the west facade and no delineation of which areas of that facade receive each cladding material. | Certain

C-3 | A4.11 Sheet 4, A4.12 Sheet 6; Specifications page 3 | Exterior detail drawings consistently call out "VENTED FIBERBOARD SOFFIT" at all overhang conditions, while the project specifications require "Hardiesoffit panels, Smooth, Primed" — fiber cement (Hardiesoffit) and wood-based fiberboard are different materials with different moisture performance characteristics. | Certain

C-4 | A4.00 E-1 Wall Assembly; G0.11 Code Summary; Specifications page 2 | The E-1 CMU wall assembly on A4.00 shows "1/2" RIGID INSULATION @ CRAWL SPACE" on the interior face of the foundation wall, providing approximately R2.5; the code summary requires crawl space wall R=10 (interior) and the project specifications state "Stem Wall: 2" rigid XPS = R10" — the drawn assembly provides less than one-quarter of the required insulation value. | Certain

C-5 | A4.00 E-4 Wall Assembly; Specifications page 4 | The project specifications describe the wall insulation system as "R21 5.5" fiberglass batts + R6 1" Insulated ZIP Board," where Insulated ZIP Board is a structural sheathing product combining OSB and rigid foam; wall assembly E-4 on A4.00 shows conventional 1/2" plywood sheathing plus a separate 1-1/2" rigid insulation board — these are not equivalent assemblies and the drawing does not use the specified product. | High

C-6 | A7.01 Sheet 1, Kitchen North Elevation; Specifications page 6 | Kitchen elevation A7.01/1 dimensions the backsplash as "SS-1 6MM," specifying a 6mm thickness, while the project specifications list backsplash SS-1 thickness as "TBD" — the drawings establish a thickness the specifications explicitly leave unresolved. | High

C-7 | A4.01 R-1 Roof Assembly | The R-1 roof assembly does not include a roofing underlayment in its layer description anywhere between the plywood sheathing and the standing seam metal roof panels; the project specifications require the roof to be "walkable with either underlayment support and/or sufficient gauge thickness," indicating an underlayment is contemplated but it is absent from the drawn assembly. | High

C-8 | A8.01 Door Schedule, Door 15-1; Specifications page 2 | The project specifications describe the garage door as a "Clopay Modern Steel Door - Grooved with Long Panel Windows," indicating a door with integral glazing panels, but the door schedule on A8.01 lists the garage door material simply as "METAL" with no notation of glazing — if the Clopay door has windows, this affects the door's thermal and visual performance characteristics. | Moderate

---

### D — Building Envelope and Building Science

D-1 | A4.00 E-1 Wall Assembly | The E-1 CMU foundation wall assembly shows only a parge coat as the exterior below-grade treatment, with no waterproofing membrane, drainage board, or drain mat on the exterior face of the CMU below grade; a parge coat alone is not a waterproofing system and is insufficient for below-grade moisture control in a high-rainfall climate zone (4A, central NC). | High

D-2 | A4.00 E-1 Wall Assembly; A4.01 R-1 Roof Assembly | The CMU crawl space wall assembly shows 1/2" rigid insulation on the interior face (approximately R2.5), while the code requires R-10 at crawl space walls; this is separately confirmed by the project specifications requiring 2" XPS — the assembly as drawn will not meet code thermal requirements for the enclosed crawl space. | Certain

D-3 | A4.01 R-1 Roof Assembly | The R-1 roof assembly places the weather barrier layer below the plywood sheathing (between the plywood and the TJI joists) rather than above the sheathing between the sheathing and the rigid insulation; placing the weather/air barrier below the structural sheathing means any moisture entering through the standing seam roof, the air gap, or the rigid insulation layer will be trapped at the plywood with no drainage path. | High

D-4 | A4.11 Sheet 5, Base Exterior | The base exterior detail (A4.11/5) shows the transition from exterior cladding to the foundation, with parge coat and insect screen but no visible capillary break or through-wall flashing at the wood sill plate level to prevent moisture wicking from the CMU to the wood framing above. | Moderate

---

### E — Code and Life Safety

E-1 | A3.22 Sheet 2, Section Stairs South | The south stair section between Level 1 and Level 2 shows 8 equal risers over a 10'-0" floor-to-floor height, which produces a riser height of 15" — exceeding the IRC maximum riser height of 7-3/4" by nearly double and rendering the stair geometry as labeled non-compliant. | Certain

E-2 | A2.01A Enlarged South Plan; A8.01 Window and Door Schedules | Second Bedroom (Room 09) is a sleeping room on Level 1; the floor plan shows window types "9" (3'-6" × 6'-0" FIXED) adjacent to this room and the window schedule confirms type 9 is fixed; no operable window or exterior door is assigned to Room 09 in either schedule, and fixed windows cannot serve as emergency escape and rescue openings as required by IRC for sleeping rooms. | High

E-3 | A2.02 Level 2 Plan; A8.01 Window Schedule | Guest Bedroom (Room 17) on Level 2 is a sleeping room; the Level 2 plan does not clearly assign operable window types to Room 17 that would meet IRC minimum emergency escape and rescue opening requirements (minimum 5.7 sq ft net clear area, 24" net clear height, 20" net clear width, maximum 44" sill height). | High

E-4 | G0.11 Code Summary | The code summary lists all construction components including floor construction as "0 HR," which does not acknowledge the IRC requirement for physical separation between an attached garage and habitable spaces using 1/2" gypsum board on the garage side of the separation wall and ceiling — this material requirement exists independently of fire-rating and is not called out in the wall assemblies adjacent to the garage. | High

E-5 | A3.22; A2.01B | No guardrail height is dimensioned at the Level 2 stair landing or open floor edge in any plan, section, or detail; the IRC requires a minimum 36" guardrail at floor edges open more than 30" above the floor below, and 42" at commercial or open-sided walking surfaces — compliance cannot be confirmed from the documents. | High

E-6 | A5.01 Enlarged Deck Plan; A5.10 Deck Elevations | The enlarged deck plan and sections note the south edge of the pool as having no railing ("south pool edge open"), while three other sides have 48" cable railings; local pool barrier ordinances in NC typically require complete enclosure of pools, and the absence of a barrier on the south edge should be confirmed with the authority having jurisdiction. | Moderate

---

### F — Constructability

F-1 | A7.10 Sheet 1, Section Reading Nook | The reading nook bunk alcove section shows a full-height built-in structure spanning from Level 1 to Level 2 with a large fixed window in the exterior wall, but no detail shows how the bunk structure bears at the floor, connects to the exterior wall assembly, or is coordinated with the Level 2 floor framing above, and no sheet A8.10 (referenced in the specifications) exists in the set to provide this information. | High

F-2 | A4.13 Sheet 5, Section Porch Overhang | The porch overhang detail shows tube steel framing for the canopy structure but does not show base plate connections, anchor bolt conditions, or how the steel tube columns bear at the foundation below — the structural connection is entirely absent from the architectural details and no coordination note directs the reader to structural drawings. | High

F-3 | A2.03 Roof Plan | A solar array covering a large portion of the north wing roof is shown on the roof plan with a note routing cables to a wall penetration, but no mounting hardware, attachment, flashing, waterproofing, or structural loading details for the solar panel array are provided anywhere in the architectural drawing set. | High

F-4 | A4.13 Sheets 1–2, Corner Window Jambs | The corner window jamb details at the Reading Nook (A4.13/1) and Kitchen (A4.13/2) show two window frames meeting directly at the building corner with only fiber cement trim between them, but no structural post, king stud, or corner column is shown at the point where the window frames terminate — this corner requires structural support that is architecturally undetailed. | High

F-5 | A4.00 Wall Assembly W-4, Interior Wall 2X4 Wood Veneer | Wall assembly W-4 shows "WD-4 / 1/2" WOOD BLOCKING AS REQUIRED / 2X4 STUDS / 1/2" GYPSUM BOARD"; the WD-4 (1×6 T&G boards) is applied over blocking on the exterior face of the stud wall, adding approximately 1-1/4" to 1-1/2" to the wall thickness, which is not reflected in the plan dimension notation "PLAN DIM" and will conflict with door frame and trim alignments at openings in W-4 walls. | High

F-6 | A2.01B, Enlarged North Plan; A4.00 | The garage (Room 15) has concrete control joint locations shown as dashed lines on the plan, but the joints are not dimensioned or coordinated with column or wall locations, leaving the joint spacing and alignment undefined for the concrete contractor. | Moderate

F-7 | A5.20 Sheet 1, Section Deck | The deck section references "SEE STRUCTURAL SET FOR DECK STRUCTURE" for the elevated wood deck over the pool mechanical area; the pool spec states the contractor shall prepare subgrade and provide utilities to the pool pad, but the deck structure above the pool is entirely deferred to absent structural drawings with no dimensional information in the architectural set for the deck framing depth or bearing conditions. | High

---

### G — Completeness and Clarity

G-1 | Cover Sheet Sheet List; A5.20 | Sheet A5.11 (listed in the index as "SECTIONS, DECK") does not exist in the drawing set; the deck section drawings are on sheet A5.20, which is not listed in the cover sheet index — cross-references within the drawing set correctly point to A5.20, confirming the index was not updated when the sheet was renumbered. | Certain

G-2 | Specifications page 5; Drawing Set | Sheet A8.10, referenced in the project specifications for the reading nook custom bunk bed detail, does not exist in the drawing set; this is information necessary to construct a significant built-in feature and its absence creates a gap in the contract documents. | Certain

G-3 | A8.01 Room Finish Schedule | The Comments column for Rooms 07 (Master Bathroom), 11 (Bathroom), and 18 (Guest Bathroom) contains the text "FOR SHOWER FINISHES SEE" with no sheet or detail number provided, making shower tile selection and layout unresolvable solely from the finish schedule. | Certain

G-4 | A3.01 West Elevation; A4.01 | Roof assembly R-2 is called out on the west elevation but is not defined anywhere in the drawing set, leaving a complete roof assembly undefined. | Certain

G-5 | A2.01A Enlarged South Plan | The note "IN FLOOR HYDRONIC HEATING SYSTEM, BIDDER DESIGN LAYOUT" defers system design to the contractor with no performance criteria, output requirements, tubing type, or floor assembly accommodation provided in either the drawings or specifications — this is insufficient for competitive bidding and will generate significant RFIs. | Certain

G-6 | Specifications page 4; Drawing Set | The project specifications require acoustic insulation in the interior wall between Master Bedroom and Second Bedroom and in second bathroom walls shared with hallway and kitchen, but no wall type in the assembly schedule (A4.00) is designated as acoustically treated, and no plans tag those specific walls with a wall type that includes acoustic insulation. | Certain

G-7 | A6.01 Lighting Fixture Schedule | All manufacturer and model fields in the lighting fixture schedule are listed as "TBD" with no allowance dollar amount established anywhere in the drawing set or specifications, leaving the entire lighting package cost basis undefined for bidders across 70+ recessed fixtures and all other fixture types. | High

G-8 | A3.22 Stair Sections | The stair sections on A3.22 show two different views (East and South) of what appear to be the same stair but with different riser counts (10 vs. 8), making it impossible to determine the actual stair configuration from the two conflicting sections — a third view or annotated plan of the stair is needed. | Certain

G-9 | A2.01A, A2.01B Enlarged Plans | Keynote callout numbers (e.g., "2C," "3C," "7C") appear on the enlarged plans adjacent to window openings but these keynote numbers do not appear in any keynote legend on those sheets — it is not clear whether these are window tags, detail references, or a different notation system. | High

G-10 | Specifications pages 6–7 | The project specifications mention geothermal ground source heat pump as "under consideration" and note that the conventional split system is a fallback — at the bid set stage this leaves a fundamental mechanical system undefined; the architectural crawl space layout, equipment space, and utility penetrations would differ materially between the two options. | High

G-11 | A2.00 Crawl Space Plan; A2.01A | The hot water heater location is noted on A2.00 with "HOT WATER (MINIMUM DISTANCE TO KITCHEN AND 2 BATHS)" but neither a specific room/location nor a rough-in dimension is provided; with two Rinnai tankless units specified, neither unit's location is dimensioned or confirmed against the minimum-distance design intent. | High

G-12 | A4.00 Wall Assemblies | Wall type W-3 (Interior Wall, Insulated) is defined on A4.00 but is not visibly tagged on any interior wall on the Level 1 or Level 2 floor plans — it is unclear which specific interior walls receive this assembly, including the acoustically sensitive walls between bedrooms described in the specifications. | High

---

## Summary

This is a conceptually strong and architecturally ambitious residential project, but the construction documents exhibit significant coordination failures that would generate a high volume of RFIs and change orders before and during construction. The drawing set and specifications were not reconciled prior to issue: the window schedule specifies Marvin Ultrex while the specifications require Andersen 400 Series — a fundamental product conflict affecting every window in the project. The exterior cladding specification simultaneously assigns two different products to the west facade. The crawl space insulation as drawn achieves approximately R2.5 against a code requirement of R10 and a specification requirement of R10 using 2" XPS. The stair geometry as labeled on A3.22 is geometrically impossible, with implied riser heights of 15" on the south section, and the riser count and rise dimensions on the east section produce a total rise that does not match the labeled floor-to-floor dimension. Several referenced sheets are missing entirely (A8.10) or exist under a different number than the index states (A5.11 vs. A5.20). The three most critical areas of concern are: **(1) the specification-drawing product conflicts** (windows, cladding, insulation) that cannot be resolved without owner direction; **(2) the stair geometry**, which must be re-detailed before structural framing begins; and **(3) the crawl space wall insulation**, where the drawn assembly fails to meet code by a factor of four and must be corrected before the foundation work is permitted.
